lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: Now near enough: your leavy screens throw down,

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: And show like those you are. You, worthy uncle,

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: Shall, with my cousin, your right-noble son,

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: Lead our first battle: worthy Macduff and we

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: Shall take upon’s what else remains to do,

lines.speaker: SIWARD
lines.text: Do we but find the tyrant’s power to-night,

lines.speaker: SIWARD
lines.text: Let us be beaten, if we cannot fight.

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: Make all our trumpets speak; give them all breath,

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: Those clamorous harbingers of blood and death.

stage_directions.text: Drum and colours. Enter Malcolm, old Siward, Macduff, and their Army, with boughs.

annotations.title: The order to drop the branches
annotations.body: Eleven lines. Malcolm tells his soldiers to throw down the branches they have been carrying — the prophecy has served its purpose, and now the army must be seen. The scene is the precise inverse of 5.4 (the order to cut branches). Shakespeare frames the defeat of equivocation as two short scenes bracketing the 'tomorrow' speech: 5.4 prepares the trick, 5.6 reveals it. The pivot is Macbeth's speech in 5.5.

options: The reveal — soldiers drop the branches they used to disguise their numbers in 5.4
